title: The icon in the tab
why.empty: The editor is empty. You deleted the page instead of adding an icon. Bring the code back.
why.not-closed: A tag is open and never closed. (<link> does not count — it has no closing tag.)
why.no-link: There is still no icon. The tag is <link>, and rel says what kind of file it is: rel="icon".
hint2.no-link: Two attributes are needed. One says WHAT the file is, the other says WHERE it is.
hint3.no-link:
— no closing tag, like and
.
why.no-href: The tag is there and says it is an icon, but it does not say which one. href is missing or empty.
why.not-in-head: The icon is not in the <head>. You put it in the <body>. The browser will take it silently — it is forgiving — but that is not where it belongs.
hint2.not-in-head: <head> is for things that are not seen on the page. The icon is seen in the TAB, not on the page.
hint3.not-in-head: Everything that says something ABOUT the page — title, encoding, icon — lives in the head. Everything that IS the page lives in the body.
hint4.not-in-head: Move the <link> between <head> and </head>, next to the <title>.
why.lost-alt: The image's alt is gone. The task is the icon, not deleting things. Do not undo what you built.
blocks.0.text: Look at the tabs in your own browser. Every one has an icon. Google has a G, YouTube the red triangle.
blocks.1.text: Your tab has a blank sheet of paper. That is the default icon — it says "nobody bothered with this site".
blocks.4.text: <link> connects the page to an external file. rel says what kind of file it is — here, an icon. href says where it is.
blocks.5.text: Another tag with no closing, again in the <head>. This is starting to feel familiar.
blocks.7.text: Open twenty tabs and look. You are not reading the titles — you are recognising the icons. The brain is faster with pictures than with letters.
blocks.8.text: If your site has no icon, it is one of many blank sheets. The person who opened it will not find their way back.
blocks.9.text: And the same goes for bookmarks, the phone's home screen, and history.
blocks.11.text: It used to be called favicon.ico and had to be exactly 16 by 16 pixels. Today a PNG does the job and you need not fuss.
blocks.12.text: If you want it perfect, you add several sizes — a phone wants a bigger one for the home screen. But one is enough to start.
blocks.13.text: A small thing. Without it a site looks unfinished, and nobody can quite say why.
blocks.14.text: Your turn. Add the icon and look at the preview's tab.
